text: This is a complex total laparoscopic myomectomy in a patient with a fifteen centimeter left brow ligament myoma. We will show our tip and tricks for this kind of surgery. In these complex cases it's important to have a strategy

The surgeon navigates around the cervical corporeal myoma, which completely encases the left round ligament, highlighting the challenge of controlling the left uterine artery at its origin due to the myoma's position.

The surgeon performs an adnexectomy by cutting the round ligament, left fallopian tube, and utero-ovarian ligament using a bipolar device for coagulation, aiming for a bloodless field to improve visibility of the large myoma. The left adnexa is removed to access the myoma.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, revealing the large veins surrounding the myoma. The dissection progresses toward the bladder, providing a clear view of the sizable myoma.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control the large vein surrounding the myoma, aiming to reduce the risk of hemorrhage. The peritoneum is carefully dissected, with smooth movements to avoid damaging the vein.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to control the large vena surrounding the myoma, followed by cutting the myometrial capsule. Traction and counter-traction techniques are applied to increase the mobility of the myoma, while opening the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The ureter is positioned below the myoma, reducing the risk of damage during the procedure. The surgeon carefully identifies the correct plane to safely extract the myoma from its bed without bleeding.

The surgeon continues anterior capsular dissection of the myoma, aiming to control over fifty percent of the myometrial capsule. A 10mm laparoscopic forceps is used to medially extract the myoma from its bed, with careful traction near the external iliac vessel. The trocar positions are visibly higher than usual.

The surgeon completes the extraction of a large myoma and performs hemostasis on the left uterine artery. The medial insertion of the myoma is cut to improve visibility of the residual uterus. Due to the uterine manipulator being found in the myoma bed, a supracervical hysterectomy is initiated, involving cutting the round ligament, tube, and utero-ovarian ligament. The left lateral aspect of the uterine isthmus is cut, and the right uterine artery is controlled and cut, progressively completing the supracervical hysterectomy for better control of the radial cervix.

The surgeon completes the supracervical hysterectomy, leaving only the posterior part of the uterus to be cut. The residual cervix is clearly visible, allowing for a safe trachelectomy. The large volume of the uterus and myoma previously complicated the bladder dissection, but it can now be performed safely.

The surgeon pushes the bladder down, revealing the residual myoma. A classical colpotomy is performed using a monopolar hook to remove the entire residual cervix, cutting the left, posterior, and right aspects of the vagina. Due to the large size of the uterus and myoma, an intra-abdominal hemisection is performed using a chardonnay knife for morselation, facilitating the vaginal extraction of uterine and myoma pieces. The 15mm myoma is cut into smaller pieces for safe removal without vaginal damage.

The surgeon uses a large 10mm forceps to tract and remove myoma pieces vaginally, minimizing the risk of vaginal trauma. The forceps, typically used in gallbladder surgery, effectively facilitates the extraction of the myoma pieces.

The surgeon identifies and controls the left ureter, ensuring its normal rotation is visible. The vaginal closure is performed using a Vicryl zero suture, incorporating the previous myoma bed into the closure. An extracorporeal knotting technique, known as the rather knot, is used to secure the suture.

The surgeon begins suturing the lateral aspect of the vagina, ensuring the entire mucosa is included in the stitches. The posterior mucosa appears deeper than usual due to the myoma location. The myoma bed is incorporated into the suture, using four or five stitches with extracorporeal or intracorporeal knotting techniques to close the vaginal cuff.

The surgeon completes the last knot using an intracorporeal knotting technique. Due to the large volume of the left myoma, a left adnexal pexy is performed to stabilize the adnexa and reduce the risk of ovarian torsion.
Thank you for our new resident that help us for this kind of surgery. I hope this video was interesting for our colleague and see you later.
